author: @tehlaser
text:
Small “yes, and”: 120 volts AC usually means 120 volts AC RMS*. The peaks hit about 170 volts. This is important when choosing a diode for your rectifier. You want one rated to handle the peak voltage.

*RMS stands for root-mean-square. It’s a way of calculating an “average” voltage over the whole sine wave. An ordinary average (arithmetic mean) wouldn’t work, because that’s just zero. All the positive volts would cancel out the negative volts. The squaring step in RMS conveniently gets rid of the negative numbers. RMS in particular, however, also makes calculating power easier for AC. For simple (read, resistive) loads, you can just pretend AC is DC at the RMS voltage and you’ll get the right answer when you multiply volts and amps to get watts, for example. Gotta be careful if you’re doing anything fancy though. Even a dimmer switch can trip you up.

author: @tridsonline
text: 👍🏻 Your liquid diode (valve) reminds me of a challenge in a year-end exam, to translate the bidirectional movement of _air_ in a cylinder that is caused by sea waves rising and falling within it, when it is positioned vertically (and fixed in relation to the earth) at the surface of the water. The idea was that they wanted to rectify the ebb and flow of air driven by sea waves into a mono-directional flow to drive an electrical generator, in the simplest, cheapest way possible.

author: @videogenie1236
text:
Hi Robert, I think you do a great job on your video explanations. However, in this video, your explanation of 240v ac wrong 
Here is the correct way to explain it.


Creating 240V AC 
To create a 240V AC RMS voltage mathematically, you need to understand the concept of RMS voltage and the waveform you're working with. In the case of a pure sinusoidal AC waveform, which is common in household electricity, you can use the following formula:

�RMS=�peak2VRMS​=2​Vpeak​​

Where:

�RMSVRMS​ is the RMS voltage.

�peakVpeak​ is the peak voltage of the sinusoidal waveform.

For a 240V RMS voltage:

�peak=240×2≈339.4VVpeak​=240×2​≈339.4V

So, to create a 240V AC RMS voltage mathematically, you would generate a sinusoidal waveform with a peak voltage of approximately 339.4V. This waveform would oscillate between +339.4V and -339.4V, and when you calculate the RMS value using the formula above, it would yield an RMS voltage of 240V.

Generating such a waveform typically involves the use of specialized electronic equipment like generators, inverters, or amplifiers, depending on your specific application. The waveform's frequency (e.g., 50Hz or 60Hz for household electricity) also needs to be considered. The exact method and components used will depend on your engineering or electrical requirements.

Compliments of chatgbt.

author: @AdricM
text: humm..  in the states its baking soda,   not baking powder.  baking powder also has tartaric acid or other things... im gonna have to look around and see what the chemistry is on this.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: Yes a dry acid with the bicarbonate in stochiometric ratio. No gas producing reaction until water is added .

replies.author: @AdricM
replies.text: @philip5940 still just curious if it is baking soda or baking powder hes using?

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: @AdricM baking powder is for scones and things and makes the dough rise because it produces bubbles of CO2 when water is added. Baking soda is pretty much pure sodium bicarbonate or it's called simply bicarbonate. Baking soda will get confused with baking powder.  The baking powder isn't pure bicarbonate but has dry acid added such as tartaric or there's a phosphoric acid partial salt compound which is commonly used today as a dry acid , and the dry acids are inactive until water is added. Bicarbonates and acid produce bubbles of co2.. Another confusion in the kitchen is corn flour which can be actually corn flour or it can be very fine wheat flour . Tin foil is actually aluminium foil . There's another kitchen confusion which has slipped my memory just now .

replies.author: @AdricM
replies.text: @philip5940 indeed im aware, but rob says baking powder,  and then later says bicarbonate. im just curious which he meant.
